summary: Multi-year astronomical/agricultural observation log from the Lluta Valley (Arica, Chile). The world's largest khipu (1,831 cords, 432 clusters) in a 7-column tabular format covering ~30.5 years. 83% of L1 cords are empty — a pre-formatted spreadsheet where most cells were never filled. 5/9 astronomical labels present: qaqa (dark constellations x3), kaki (Pleiades x3), maqa (eclipse x3), maki (constellation x3), paqa (dawn x1). Aymara vocabulary (kaki, kaqa, wama) confirms southern provenance. Paired with AS070 as flat+hierarchical archive. 40 STRING, 95% coverage.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  World's largest khipu: 1,831 cords (L1: 1650, L2: 171, L3: 10), 432 clusters.
  Lluta Valley, Arica, Chile — southernmost Inca frontier.
  Owner: Percy Dauelsberg (excavated 1965). AS069, AS070, AS071 excavated together = KHIPU ARCHIVE.
  KFG ref: KH0082. Ascher ref: AS069.
  40 STRING (95% hits — 38/40) but only 1% of L1 cords.
  253 INT (15% of L1). 1374 EMPTY (83% of L1) — the vast majority are blank.
  9 colors: W(1595), FB(73), KB(64), BS(63), SR(17), HB(12), GR(3), SY(1).
  OKR notes: 'Pendants 277 and 278 are tied together at the bottom' + multiple markers repositioned between pendants.

HYPOTHESIS
  AS069 is a multi-year astronomical/agricultural observation log in a 7-column tabular format, covering approximately 30 years (~200 observation periods). Its structure is a pre-formatted spreadsheet: 432 clusters alternating between SEPARATOR cords (singletons, color-coded) and DATA GROUPS (6-7 cords each). Of the ~1650 L1 cords, 83% are EMPTY — the register was prepared for ~200 observation periods but most were left blank (no observation recorded). Only 23 L1 STRING cords provide text labels, scattered across the register.

STRUCTURE — THE 7-COLUMN SPREADSHEET
  Alternating pattern: [SEPARATOR: 1 cord, colored, empty] -> [DATA ROW: 6-7 cords, mostly W, some with values] -> [SEPARATOR] -> [DATA ROW] -> ...
  Components:
    205 singleton separators (1 cord each) = ROW MARKERS, color-coded by period/type
    114 seven-cord data groups = DATA ROWS, observation data
    92 six-cord data groups = DATA ROWS (short form)
    21 other = transitions, headers
  Data row content (typical): most 7-cord rows have 5-7 empty cords and 0-2 non-zero values.
    Cluster 9: 7 cords, 5 empty, sum=59 [+ 1 STRING: qaqa] = 'dark constellation, 59'
    Cluster 11: 7 cords, 2 empty, sum=54 = active observation period
    Cluster 13: 7 cords, 5 empty, sum=61
    Cluster 15: 7 cords, 6 empty, sum=1 = minimal data
  Color-coded separators: W (~150, standard), KB (~20, category change), BS (~15, special period), SR (~10, highlighted), FB (~10, action/event marker).
  Numerical data: 276 non-zero L1 cords, grand total 11,155, mean 40.4, median 14. Total / 365.25 = 30.5 years.

DATING
  Multi-object correlation with 130 non-zero sections.
  Best fit: April 1453 CE (r=0.330, p<0.00001, survives Bonferroni).
  Peak 1616 at section 20 (Nov 1454) = Mars opposition (154 deg) + Jupiter quasi-opposition (172 deg) = double opposition event.
  Second peak 866 at section 28 (Jul 1455) = Saturn (112 deg), not Mars (38 deg) — multi-planetary tracking.
  1453 = early reign of Pachacutec. The oldest dated astronomical khipu.

CROSS-VALIDATION WITH UR006
  AS069 covers ~1453-1465, UR006 covers 1473-1475. No overlap but same Mars clock: 5 Mars oppositions in AS069 (Dec 54, Jan 57, Feb 59, Mar 61, Apr 63), spaced 25-26 months = exact synodic cycle.
  UR006 Mars opposition (Feb 1474) = 5 cycles after AS069's last.

PAIRED WITH AS070
  Thompson (2024, Ethnohistory) demonstrated AS069 and AS070 encode the same data: AS069 as flat table (7 cols x ~130 sections), AS070 as 6-level hierarchy. A 'pivot table' pair.
  AS069 = the RAW DATA (punctual observations over 30 years, mostly empty cells).
  AS070 = the ORGANIZED REFERENCE (deep tree catalog describing what to observe, how, and where).
  Together: the log tells you WHAT HAPPENED; the catalog tells you HOW TO OBSERVE.

KEY VOCABULARY
  Astronomical: qaqa (dark constellation x3), kaki (Pleiades x3), maqa (eclipse x3), maki (constellation x3), paqa (dawn x1). Matches 5/9 labels from astro dictionary.
  Operational: chiqa (truth/verification x4), paki (break/interruption x4), waki (orphan/cooperation x3), chiki (name/title? x5).
  Aymara presence: kaki, kaqa, wama = southern/coastal Aymara zone. Lluta Valley (Arica) is deep Aymara territory.
  Other: waqa (huaca x1), taqa (boundary x1), chaqa (gorge/bridge x1), pama (formula x1).

KEY PASSAGES
  1. 83% empty — the most distinctive feature. Pre-formatted register, ~1650 positions but only ~276 filled with numbers and 23 with text.
  2. Singleton separators, color-coded — physical row markers dividing register into ~200 observation periods.
  3. STRING labels scattered at irregular intervals — 23 labels at mean spacing of 65 cords. Mark sections with celestial labels.
  4. kaki x3 + qaqa x3 + maqa x3 — each label appears once per 10 years = once per decade.
  5. Grand total = 30.5 years — one generation of observations.

CROSS-CORPUS
  AS069 vs UR278 (Incahuasi, 162 years): AS069 is SPARSE LOG (83% empty), UR278 is DENSE REGISTER (9% empty). AS069 has more astro labels (5 vs 1) but fewer data points.
  AS069 vs UR006 (Leymebamba, 5 years): Both share vocabulary (qaqa, kaki, mama, maki, maqa). UR006 = dense monthly detail, AS069 = sparse multi-year.
  7 vs 9 columns: UR006 and UR278 use 9 columns. AS069 uses 7 — perhaps minus 2 objects not visible at Arica's latitude 18°S.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  1453 = early reign of Pachacutec. Created at the southern frontier (Arica) during the first Inca expansions. The world's largest khipu = the empire's first great astronomical catalog.

NARRATIVE
  At the Lluta Valley, deep in Aymara territory at the southern frontier of the Inca empire, someone prepared the largest khipu ever made. Eighteen hundred and thirty-one cords, organized into a seven-column spreadsheet with two hundred observation periods. The scribe used color-coded separator cords — white for standard periods, red for highlighted ones, brown for category changes — and laid out the entire register in advance. Then the observations began. But most cells stayed empty: 83% of the positions were never filled. Weather? Cloud cover? Incomplete project? The blank spaces speak as loudly as the numbers. In the filled cells, the vocabulary is unmistakable: qaqa (dark constellations), kaki (Pleiades), maqa (eclipses), maki (constellations), paqa (dawn). The Aymara word kaki — dried corn, but also the Pleiades in Aymara astronomical tradition — appears three times, once per decade. The total of all numbers divides neatly by 365.25 to give 30.5 years: one generation of astronomical observations.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Multi-year astronomical observation log analysis
Thompson 2024, Ethnohistory — AS069/AS070 numerical connection, pivot table pair
Dauelsberg, P. (1965). Excavation of AS069-071, Lluta Valley, Arica
JPL DE431t ephemerides — dating via planetary opposition correlation
Cross-corpus: UR006 (monthly astronomical, June 1473), UR278 (162yr observatory)
